title: Burien
name: Burien
metaDescription: Burien 1031 exchange coordination for owners of airport-adjacent industrial, corridor retail, and working multifamily near Sea-Tac's flight-path and noise overlays.

sections.html: Burien sits close enough to Sea-Tac International Airport that almost every commercial procurement decision here has to account for the airport's footprint in some way, whether that means ground transport and air-cargo tenants along the industrial corridor or noise overlay restrictions on residential-adjacent parcels. Retail along Ambaum Boulevard SW and 1st Avenue South rounds out a submarket that trades at a discount to Seattle proper while still benefiting from steady airport-driven demand.

sections.heading: Airport-Adjacent Industrial and Corridor Retail Scope
sections.html:
Warehouse and flex buildings near the SeaTac and Burien boundary serve ground transportation, parts, and logistics tenants supporting airport operations, and vacancy in this category tends to track airport passenger and cargo volume rather than the broader industrial market. Retail along Ambaum Boulevard SW and 1st Avenue South is largely service and value-oriented, centered on the redeveloped Burien Town Square area, with older multifamily stock filling in the surrounding blocks.

Burien's discount to Seattle and Bellevue pricing has drawn value-add multifamily buyers over the past several years, and much of the city's older garden-style apartment stock has changed hands at least once as a result, which gives an exchange buyer a reasonably deep pool of recent comparable sales to underwrite against.

sections.heading: The Procurement Categories Burien Investors Actually Pursue
sections.html:
An identification package built for Burien typically draws from a handful of recurring specifications:

  • Airport-support industrial and flex buildings near the SeaTac boundary
  • Service and value retail along Ambaum Boulevard SW and 1st Avenue South
  • Mixed-use retail and residential within the Burien Town Square redevelopment area
  • Older garden-style apartment buildings subject to the statewide rent-increase cap
  • DST interests for owners preferring passive exposure to airport-adjacent demand

sections.heading: Underwriting Noise Overlay and Flight Path Constraints
sections.html:
Parcels within Sea-Tac's designated noise overlay zones can carry use restrictions or require sound insulation improvements that affect both cost and permitted redevelopment, and a buyer should confirm a target property's overlay status with the Port of Seattle's noise remedy program before finalizing underwriting. Ground lease and easement terms tied to airport operations should also be reviewed closely, since some Burien industrial parcels carry legacy agreements from the airport's prior expansions.

A buyer should request the property's full noise remedy program history from the seller, including whether the building already received sound insulation funding, since a property that has not yet been through the program may face a longer approval queue if the new owner later applies on their own.

sections.heading: Sequencing Identification Near an Airport-Driven Market
sections.html:
Because airport-support industrial in Burien tends to lease quickly once vacated, an exchanging owner should have comparable candidates already vetted before the 45-day identification clock starts, rather than beginning the search from scratch after closing the relinquished property. A three-property rule submittal is usually sufficient here given the manageable size of the airport-adjacent inventory pool.

An owner targeting the Town Square mixed-use area should build in extra sourcing time relative to the industrial category, since this redevelopment zone has fewer comparable transactions to draw from and pricing can move quickly once a building becomes available.

sections.heading: Full Carry Cost Considerations for Burien Acquisitions
sections.html:
Insurance quotes on Burien industrial product should reflect proximity to airport operations, which can affect underwriting differently than a comparable building further from the flight path. A buyer should also confirm whether any sound insulation or noise remedy work has already been completed on a target property, since unfinished mitigation can become the new owner's cost and timeline obligation rather than a resolved item at closing.

Property tax figures on Burien acquisitions should be checked against King County's current assessed value rather than the seller's prior tax bill, since assessed values in this submarket have moved with the broader value-add buying activity described earlier, and a stale figure can understate the actual carrying cost a new owner should expect.

faqs.q: Does proximity to Sea-Tac Airport affect underwriting on a Burien property?
faqs.a: Yes. Parcels within the airport's noise overlay zones can carry use restrictions or sound insulation requirements, and industrial vacancy in the airport-support category tends to track passenger and cargo volume rather than the general industrial cycle.

faqs.q: What kind of commercial property is most common in Burien?
faqs.a: Airport-support industrial and flex buildings, service and value retail along Ambaum Boulevard SW and 1st Avenue South, and older garden-style apartment buildings. The Burien Town Square area adds mixed-use retail and residential product.

faqs.q: How does the statewide rent-increase cap affect Burien multifamily underwriting?
faqs.a: Older Burien apartment buildings are subject to the statewide cap on annual rent increases, so a lender or buyer should model rent growth against that limit rather than assume unrestricted market-rate increases going forward.

faqs.q: Can 1031 Exchange Seattle confirm a Burien property qualifies for my exchange?
faqs.a: No. This service coordinates identification, documentation, and communication among an investor's advisors. Qualification under Section 1031 is a determination made by the investor's qualified intermediary and tax advisor.

faqs.q: Should I check whether a Burien property already received noise-remedy sound insulation funding?
faqs.a: Yes. A property that has already been through the Port of Seattle's noise remedy program has resolved sound insulation ahead of time, while one that has not may face a longer approval queue if a new owner applies on their own.

faqs.q: Are Burien property tax figures reliable to use from the seller's prior tax bill?
faqs.a: Not always. Assessed values in Burien have moved as value-add buying activity has picked up, so a buyer should confirm the current King County assessed value directly rather than rely on an older figure that may understate the actual carrying cost.
